chunks.text:
Letter from Ofcom regarding mandatory age assurance requirements for services that allow pornographic content
Letter from Ofcom regarding mandatory age assurance requirements for services that allow pornographic content
URL copied
-->
Online safety
Protecting children
Online pornography
News and updates
Speeches and Comment
Published:
24 April 2025
On 24 April 2025, Ofcom published the
Protection of Children Codes of Practice and guidance
. As part of this package of regulations, from
25 July 2025
,
services in scope of the Online Safety Act and which allow pornography, must implement highly effective age assurance to stop under-18s encountering that content.
The letter below has been sent out to hundreds of services whose principal purpose is to host pornography. It sets out what those services need to do in more detail, provides contact information if services have questions, and explains the consequences of non-compliance.
For services which allow pornography alongside other types of content, there are more requirements under Ofcom’s Protection of Children Duties which are not covered in this letter.
Letter
Letter to Part 3 services that allow pornography
•
PDF
•
195.48 KB
•
24 April 2025
Quick guide to implementing highly effective age assurance
The Online Safety Act has introduced new rules on robust age checks that services must follow to protect children
How to comply with the Online Safety Act: responding to Ofcom’s requests for information
The Online Safety Act (OSA) makes businesses, and anyone else who operates a wide range of online services, legally responsible for keeping people (especially children) in the UK safe online.
‘Adults Only’: what to do if your online service allows pornography
If you allow pornography on your online service, this page is for you. It explains what you need to know about the Online Safety Act and what you need to check to ensure you follow the rules.
